headline: All majors underwater against the 200-day, but momentum intact and whales withdrawing coins to cold storage
paragraphs: The regime is bears-in-control, cleanly: Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, XRP, BNB, Doge, and Cardano are all trading below their 200-day simple moving averages, and none have golden crosses to suggest mean reversion is forming. Bitcoin is 48.1 percent down from its 12-month high, Ethereum 61 percent, and Cardano down 82.9 percent. This is a downtrend structure, not ambiguous. Yet the 24-hour moves show modest green across the board: BTC +0.17 percent, ETH +0.59 percent, SOL +0.75 percent, XRP +0.66 percent, DOGE +0.83 percent. Cardano is the sole major red, off 0.88 percent. The tape here is not capitulation, which would show larger single-day swings; it is quiet persistence in a bear structure. Total market cap rose 0.37 percent to 2.3 trillion, and Bitcoin dominance holds at 56.6 percent. The crowd is afraid: the Fear and Greed index reads 29, labeled Fear, with a 30-day range of 11 to 29, so today is at the upper bound of the recent fear zone. This is not panic; panic does not last 30 days. This is grinding wariness.

Momentum is sending a mixed signal against that downtrend. Bitcoin's RSI-14 sits at 55.1 with MACD above its signal line, the shape of resting upward momentum rather than acceleration. Ethereum's RSI is 60.6, also MACD-positive, suggesting it has climbed off oversold faster than Bitcoin. Solana's RSI reads 52, but MACD is below signal, the first real divergence: price is grinding sideways while the oscillator is declining. Cardano shows RSI at 47.4, deeply neutral-to-weak, though MACD is still above signal. The pattern across the board is that MACD remains positive for most majors, which means the recent small bounce has momentum behind it, but RSI values in the 40s to low 60s tell you there is no overbought condition and room for more upside without triggering a reversal signal. The downtrend is intact, but the bounce is not broken yet. That is a tension worth holding in mind: a trend lower, but momentum that has not rolled over.

Derivatives positioning reveals crowding on the long side and expensive financing on the smaller names. Bitcoin's long-to-short ratio on OKX sits at 1.41, which means for every short there are 1.41 longs: a bullish skew, but not extreme. Funding is tiny at 0.0011 percent per 8-hour period (1.3 percent annualized), so longs are not paying a steep premium to hold. Ethereum's ratio is 1.69, more skewed to longs, with negative funding of minus 0.0007 percent per 8-hour (minus 0.8 percent annualized), meaning shorts are actually paying longs, a sign shorts are fleeing and longs are entrenched. Open interest on BTC is 2.04 billion dollars and on ETH is 1.33 billion, both substantial. But look at Doge: the long-to-short ratio is 4.81 and funding is 0.0063 percent per 8-hour, or 6.9 percent annualized. XRP funds at 5.5 percent annualized with a 2.95 ratio. These smaller names are crowded with longs paying steeply to hold. Liquidations in the past 24 hours show more shorts getting blown out than longs across almost every asset: Bitcoin saw 10.4 million dollars in short liquidations versus 5.6 million in longs. Ethereum was nearly balanced at 8.0 million longs and 7.4 million shorts. Solana, XRP, and Doge all saw more longs liquidated than shorts. The smaller-cap names are fragile long crowdedness.

Whale behavior and stablecoin float are the clearest supply-demand signal. In the past 48 hours, 133 million dollars in Bitcoin moved off Kraken to cold storage, the opposite of panic selling to exchanges. That is accumulation by large holders at lower prices, a structural bid. The weekly trend in whale flows shows recent weeks: 1.52 billion, 1.28 billion, 343 million, and minus 59 million most recently. The last week turned negative, which means coins flowed onto exchanges, but before that three consecutive weeks saw net outflows, so the trend is not fresh panic. Stablecoin float sits at 306.3 billion, down 2.03 percent in the past 30 days, so dry powder is shrinking. This is a contradiction with the whale accumulation story: whales are taking coins off the market, but the cash float that typically finances fresh buying is draining. Spot ETF flows are still positive: Bitcoin received 132.3 million net inflow and Ethereum 36.7 million as of July 17, with cumulative flows of 51.4 billion and 11.1 billion respectively. Traditional finance is still adding, but the pace is moderate.

Sentiment and tape are misaligned in a telling way. The Fear and Greed gauge screams fear at 29, the crowd reads danger, yet whales are accumulating, spot ETF inflows are positive, and momentum remains above signal on most names. The liquidation data shows short-side pain more than long-side pain. This gap is usually content: either the crowd is right and the tape is late to reflect it, or the crowd is ahead of itself and will be forced to give ground. The Bitcoin network shows difficulty is rising 2.8 percent with 713 blocks until retarget, meaning hash rate climbed into this downtrend, so miner conviction remains intact. The micro-detail to watch is the funding flip on Bitcoin: if 1.3 percent annualized on BTC moves positive and stays positive, it signals longs are reaccumulating aggressively at the margin. Similarly, if whale outflows reverse back to net inflows after the recent turn negative, that is confirmation of the cold-storage story. And if stablecoin float begins to stabilize and resume growth, the structural dry powder for a move higher re-emerges. For now, the tape shows downtrend, resting momentum, and quiet accumulation by patient holders.
